speaker: interviewer
text: Gabriela Katz on May 10, 1996 in San Diego, California. Let's start talking about the time of liberation. You briefly mentioned that before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How exactly was Brunnlitz liberated and by whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brunnlitz was liberated by Russians. We were sitting there not knowing what's going on. We assume because Schindler left already. The people from the camp took him away with the car, him and his wife, and few Jews went with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were sitting in this camp not knowing what's going on with nobody there to supervise. You don't know if you can go out, if you cannot go out, finally you notice a few people left and went to Prague. Do you remember how you reacted to knowing that the war was over for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was completely I would say without any emotions. You start to think thank God that this is it. We went so-- we started to talk about leaving. We're, of course, not going to Poland, not going to be with Russian people. And my husband wanted to go to Italy, so we went to Czechoslovakia with another couple from the camp. And from there, we went to Salzburg. This was a car with the cattle, and we went then.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came to Salzburg and this couple with us, he was a German Jew. And he was going on my nerves because [SPEAKING GERMAN] to the Germans in Salzburg. What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: This means you are talking to somebody with respect. I didn't have respect for Germans in this time. So we went to such organization then in Salzburg, and they gave us the location in some old lady's house. Beautiful. Everything inside, beautiful house. She was a wife of judge who had escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were staying there, and I wanted to cook something. I went shopping for bread. And the lines were very big. I was staying there in the line, and I got some coat in Czechoslovakia. It was cold, and I was wearing this coat. And young guy behind me staying in line emptied his pen on my coat. You know they hated us so much. So I went out of the line. I came to the house, and I said, to my husband, we're going. So he said, where are we going? You wanted to go to Italy, we're going to Italy. I cannot take this Ge-- I cannot take this language. I cannot take these people anymore. So we decided to go further. So we went into the train station. All of a sudden, we started to think normally. We going train station, you know? We came to this train station in Salzburg. There was division between the Russian occupation and the American occupation. And they started to ask us we are Jews. We are coming from concentration camp. My husband wants to go to Italy to pick up his papers, diploma. So they call up from the army there, they call up an Italian fellow. He started to talk to my husband. My husband talked in Italian. Fine. Then he says, who is this? This is my wife. He starts to talk Italian to me. I didn't understand one word.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband said, but this is my wife, and she is Jewish girl from Przemysl. And they call up an American was speaking Jewish to me. And I didn't understand one word, so it's no good. The guys were standing here, and I simply went through the division. And I went to the train there, going toward Italy, Boissano, Mirano. And so we came to Italy. We had a little bit money that they gave us in Czechoslovakia. Everybody from the camp got a little bit of money. We sat down in a coffee shop in Bologna. I never will forget this. We ordered two drinks. We spent all the money. So this was our coming to Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find what happened to all your family's possessions, your home, and everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Later they wrote us letter. Of course, I talk to the lawyers. I wrote to the lawyers and even a lawyer [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] told us that the Polish government is giving us $40,000 for my husband's house. And they were supposed to give us the money. They gave us once $400 and this was it. They were bankrupt, so this was it. But they sent us always the bills for taxes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, we wrote them to keep the house. We don't want to pay the taxes and keep it, and this was it. And we didn't get anything from Poland, nothing. I got pension from Germany like everybody was getting for the health that was affected by the war and so on. But this I am getting till today. I am getting $700 per month, and this is it. Talk about the early years then with your husband and starting a family. How did the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Italy, and my husband started already to practice. And we lived in Bologna. Then we moved to Rome. My husband in Bologna went back and did his speciality in skin and venereal diseases. And then I discovered I have a cousin in Palermo, which is a physician. I knew that he was in Palermo. He was in the other Ferramonti which was an Italian concentration camp that they gave you vacation there. You know. And he was a doctor there, Ferramonti. And so we left Rome after my husband took it and we went to Palermo, Sicily. In Palermo, Sicily, I inscribed into the University of-- no, first in Bologna, then in Palermo. I wanted to finish my studies, but I couldn't do it because I got pregnant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my daughter was born in Rome, the older one. What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lia, L-I-A, Lia Katz-Robinson. And then I turn my ambitions into my two daughters-- one went into medical school here in United States, and one went to PhD in microbiology. So I used my ambitions this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, since your husband also was a survivor, could you talk about how the war experience affected you and your husband in America and raising a family and so on?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to very wonderful life after the war in Italy. But we had my daughter, and my husband decided he doesn't want to stay in Italy. No Jews in Italy, so she's going to marry-- she was nine months old, you see. She's going to marry not Jewish person.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we have a friend in Rome that we lived together in one apartment, and she was from Hungary. She was working in [NON-ENGLISH] and she was arranging all the immigrations of DPs. And she calls me up once, and she said, I'm going to put you on the list to go to the United States. And we didn't have any family, nothing. I said, don't do it. We cannot go. I'm to put you on the list anyway. And who went to United States? Us. She put us on the list. Everything was fine, and we left for New York. And the trip was terrible. And we came to New York, and we discovered that it's not so easy. They gave my husband, a [NON-ENGLISH] gave my husband information that he has to take the internship, which he didn't have to do it because he had a diploma before the war had started.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they told him, and he took a internship in Coney Island Hospital in New York, and he was there one year-- and this was-- we didn't have money at this time. The United Jewish Appeal didn't help us anymore, and it was very, very tough. I had a friend, not Jewish person, that lived in Stockholm in Brooklyn. This was really Ridgewood, and she was helping us out. In the meantime, I was very sick. I forgot about this. The minute I came to United States, I got a gall bladder attack. And I went for surgery in Coney Island Hospital. My husband was doing the internship there. So after that, I was on diet, on the gaining diet. I had to eat butter to put some-- they gave me pills, butter. I wish this was the situation now. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying in between tapes that you never talked about this much, that you don't have dreams about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: It doesn't affect you that much.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you account for that that after all this, it doesn't seem to be an issue for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It never was. I never took it as an issue. This was like this, and this is it. I usually fight the things that I could do. But if I couldn't do anything, I didn't bother. I couldn't do anything. I could do something to go and get a piece of bread, but I couldn't save myself. I don't know. I was completely like immune, just like water for me. Everything. It bothered me, for example, in Plaszow to see this, what the Jews were doing to Jews. This was a different story or to see how they killed the people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were terrible things. If I would take it inside, I wouldn't be alive, but I was immune. I made myself immune to everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find yourself feeling again after the war, to become not immune?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too busy to progress. My husband was taking the exams. I had the baby. I had many friends. The life started to build better and better. Then we moved to Syracuse, New York. And I had many friends, but this was a Polish group, but they had many friends, Americans. I tried to learn the language. I took several courses there in Syracuse University.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you suppose the war period changed you, if it did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not emotional anymore. No emotions. I went into the thinking of future. The second time that I was going to Italy back. My daughter was seven years old, the older one. I had some very good helper. I was going to leave her and my husband, go to Italy. I was there already on the third year of medical school. I needed two, and I was going to finish. But I was again pregnant, and this was it. My husband said you are not going any place, so this was it. So I two daughters. I didn't have the diploma, but I concentrated on them. My husband had a very lucrative practice in Syracuse, and this was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the war affect your Jewish values, Jewish identity in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. I realize that I'm Jewish. Does matter. If you are reform or-- We were going always to every Jewish presentation. My husband was giving a lot of money to the Jewish congress. We went three times to Israel. We did the missions and so on. I have many, many friends from my young years in Israel, not so many any more because the people were dying out. But I realize I was born in Poland, I considered myself Polish, but nobody considered my Polish. So I was Jewish living in Poland. And it was really hard to realize this because I was always very patriotic. I was going to Polish schools. I didn't have before too much to do with the Jewish way of life, but I married. When I married my husband, I was exposed to because his family was very Jewish. Let's put it this way. And his grandmother didn't come to my wedding because he is marrying not Jewish person. Some people in Poland, they were like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think the Jewish people should learn anything from what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not take anything for granted. You think. And first of all, you have to realize don't believe everything somebody tells you. And something else too, fight back. Learn to fight. Jews are not fighters, but in Israel they are. Something like this that happened in Poland couldn't happen in Israel. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: M-hm. Before we look at the family pictures, one or two other questions. Do you have anything you'd like to convey to your kids and grandkids after all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I feel sorry that I never talk to my kids about these times. Therefore, she was so anxious to come-- my husband was. But I never did, because why? What this will do? I don't know, but they were very anxious to learn. So one was buying me the books. The other one was--

speaker: interviewer
text: As a matter of fact, when my older daughter was after, I think 19, I sent her to Israel to kibbutz for whole summer. And you know what she wrote to me? You brought me up to be American. I don't like the toilet paper here. She's an American. I sent here there because I was hoping that here-- but now, they are very Jewish, much more than I was. They belong to the temple. Her daughter was now bat mitzvah. What? What would you want to hear your grandchildren and their children to learn from the story that you've told? What should they get out of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's up to them. What I can tell them? I can tell them they go to the temple. They go to Sunday school. By the way, that is organized so that they hate it. They would rather go skiing than to go to Sunday school, but my daughter made them. And my daughter knows every prayer in memory because I exposed her. I sent her to Sunday school and to the temple and everything. I cannot do anything more. But to tell them about the war, about the cruelties, about this, what was happening, I can tell them one thing. You are Jewish and you act this one. Because you are Jewish, everybody will tell you you're Jewish. It's not this that somebody tell you. You believe it. You have to belong someplace. Your cannot like German Jews-- our Germany or my Poland. I never went back to Poland after the war. I would not go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, one final question. When you saw I guess, a few years ago, do you remember what your response was, what your impression was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very well done, I said. Very well done. Yes. I think through this like like all the well-written books, this what I would like my kids and my grandkids to read to learn from. If they have lectures about the holocaust on the level of university, there's one professor in Riverside University, he's American. I forgot his name. And he has sometimes lectures on all this. He's terrific. He looks at everything from the historical point of view. But I hate to listen to a lecture. Like I went to this museum, and was lady lecturing me about this time, how she didn't have something to eat or how she lost her brother. This, I am not interested. I know all those things. But this professor, he's professor of history in Riverside and he gave a speech about this from historical point of view that was excellent because we never had this. We were in the camps. You know what I mean. I don't know if you get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I follow you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You follow me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Well, let's look at the family pictures then.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I can get up. And this is my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, no. I'm sorry. I'm on you're father. You can't tell. I should do like this so you can see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead and tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. This is my father. This picture was done some 60 years ago in Poland. You want the name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Nefstali Spinner, S-P-I-N-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Cecilia Spandorf. She was born in Przemysl, and the date of the photo is around 80 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell us about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, Marcel Spinner, S-P-I-N-N-E-R. He was born 1922, and this photo was done a few years before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Tell us about this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband's brother. His name was Barcott. He was born in Przemysl, and he was born in year '24, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband's sister, Sipora Katz, born in Przemysl and in year 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our Polish dance class in Przemysl, and the date probably would be five years before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Tell us about this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a family picture. On the right--

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't point. Go ahead, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son-in-law, Mark. This is my husband and that's me. Then this is my other son-in-law, Rob, my daughter, Darlene, my granddaughter, Ariel, my daughter, Lia, and my grandson, Charlie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Tell me where it was taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in my daughter's house in Carlsbad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how long ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are my two other grandchildren, Darlene's children. Her name is Joanna and his name is Doug.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Carlsbad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Right.
